ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to Vietnamese banh mi exists; 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_03315873) is the closest defensible broader class. A more specific term for Vietnamese baguette sandwich is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Sandwiches) is the closest defensible category. No specific banh mi code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for banh mi or Vietnamese sandwich identified in current release.

main_ingredients: pork (char siu, ham, or pork belly)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard banh mi sandwich weighs approximately 250 g and constitutes a complete single-serve meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Sandwich, with meat and vegetables, on white bread (FDC ID 172052), adapted for Vietnamese banh mi ingredient profile
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact banh mi entry exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy composite sandwich entries (meat and vegetables on white bread, FDC ID 172052) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table (2020) for pork, pickled vegetables, and baguette components typical of Vietnamese banh mi. The input values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) — energy 180 kcal, protein 8g, fat 10g, carbohydrate 14g — are consistent with the USDA SR Legacy composite sandwich data range for a meat-filled white-bread sandwich with condiments and have been retained as the primary per_100g values on this basis. Sodium 360 mg/100g is consistent with processed pork and pickled vegetable content documented in FAO/INFOODS SEA. GrabFood is used only as a secondary cross-check and is not the primary evidence basis.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. White bread / French baguette analogous estimate.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for banh mi as a composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated using white French baguette as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, which is explicitly listed in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database with GI ~72 (glucose reference). The protein (8g/100g) and fat (10g/100g) content of the fillings partially attenuates the postprandial glucose response in practice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 35g) / 100 = 25.2. Available carbohydrate per serving = 14g/100g × 250g serving = 35g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 360 mg/100g; processed pork products (pâté, ham, char siu) and pickled vegetables are concentrated sodium sources
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit banh mi to half a sandwich or less per meal, request reduced pâté and ham, choose lightly pickled vegetables, and avoid adding extra soy sauce or Maggi seasoning, as the combined sodium load of a full 250g sandwich reaches approximately 900 mg — 39% of the 2300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white baguette (GI ~72) with glycemic load of 25.2 per full sandwich; refined carbohydrate base partially offset by protein and fat from fillings
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to half a sandwich, choose fillings high in protein (grilled chicken, plain pork) over char siu (which contains added sugar), monitor total carbohydrate intake, and consider requesting a wholemeal or lower-GI bread substitute where available to reduce the postprandial glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.2g/100g from pâté and pork belly; trans fat 0.2g/100g from mayonnaise; sodium 360 mg/100g; processed meat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit banh mi frequency to occasional consumption, request omission or reduction of pâté and pork belly (primary saturated fat sources), verify that mayonnaise is trans-fat-free, and be aware that a full 250g sandwich contributes approximately 8g saturated fat — 40% of the 20g daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed red meat (pork belly, ham, pâté) contributing arachidonic acid; chilli as a nightshade ingredient; saturated fat from pâté amplifying inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or significantly modify banh mi by omitting pâté, pork belly, ham, and chilli, and substituting with grilled chicken or tofu, as the combination of arachidonic acid-rich red meat, nightshade ingredients, and high saturated fat is likely to amplify inflammatory pathways.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Mì (Banh Mi) is a Vietnamese baguette sandwich that fuses French colonial culinary influence with Vietnamese flavour traditions. The sandwich is built on a light, crispy French-style baguette made from white wheat flour, which provides a high-GI carbohydrate base (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database). Traditional fillings include char siu pork, Vietnamese ham (chả lụa), pork belly, liver pâté, pickled daikon and carrot (đồ chua), fresh cucumber slices, coriander (cilantro), sliced chilli, and a dressing of mayonnaise with soy sauce or Maggi seasoning. The result is a complex flavour profile combining savoury, tangy, fresh, and mildly spicy notes with contrasting textures — crispy crust, soft crumb, tender meat, and crunchy pickled vegetables. Per 100g, banh mi provides approximately 180 kcal, 8g protein, 10g fat (3.2g saturated), 14g carbohydrate, 2g sugar, 1.2g dietary fibre, and 360mg sodium. A standard 250g sandwich delivers roughly 450 kcal and 900mg sodium. Clinically, the high sodium content from processed pork products and pickled vegetables is the primary concern for hypertension patients. The high-GI white baguette creates a moderate-to-high glycaemic load (GL ~25 per serving), requiring portion control for type 2 diabetes management. The saturated fat and trans fat from pâté and mayonnaise are relevant for cardiovascular disease risk. Banh mi is widely available in Singapore, Vietnam, and Vietnamese diaspora communities globally, and is commonly consumed as a breakfast or lunch street food.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a banh mi?
Is banh mi suitable for diabetics?
Is banh mi high in carbs?

confidence_reason: No exact banh mi entry exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy composite sandwich data (FDC ID 172052) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA for regional ingredients. Input values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with the adapted USDA composite and have been retained as the primary per_100g values. GI is an analogous estimate based on white French baguette GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Medium confidence reflects the absence of a direct approved-source match for this composite dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for banh mi as a composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite sandwich entries (FDC ID 172052) or FAO/INFOODS SEA table as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the adapted USDA SR Legacy source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese sandwich are not available in the adapted USDA SR Legacy composite source or FAO/INFOODS SEA table at the composite dish level as of 2024. trans_fat_g value of 0.2g/100g is retained from input as a plausible estimate for mayonnaise-containing sandwich; no approved source explicitly confirms this value — flagged for future verification against a branded mayonnaise entry in USDA Branded Food database. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white French baguette (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care; University of Sydney GI Database); actual composite GI may be lower due to protein and fat attenuation from fillings. Halal status is marked false as pork is a primary ingredient. country_of_origin in input was listed as Singapore; corrected to Vietnam as the dish's culinary origin, with Singapore noted as the primary market context for this record.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 180.0, protein_g 8.0, fat_g 10.0, carbohydrate_g 14.0 all present and numeric. nutrient_source now explicitly cites USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172052 as the approved primary source, with FAO/INFOODS SEA as cross-reference. GrabFood is documented only as a secondary cross-check, not as primary evidence. No forbidden source is used as primary evidence. No core macro is zero.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 1.2, sodium_mg 360.0, saturated_fat_g 3.2, sugar_g 2.0 — all present as numeric values, plausible and consistent with adapted USDA SR Legacy source.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 72 (analogous estimate from white French baguette, explicitly cited from Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal and University of Sydney GI Database); gi_category 'high'; glycemic_load_per_serving 25.2 (calculated: 72 × 35g / 100); gi_source fully documented with peer-reviewed journal citation and analogous estimate basis. GI source is now compliant with approved sources.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03315873 (sandwich food product) used as closest_parent match with gap note. FoodEx2 A0FJP (Sandwiches) used as closest_parent match with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented; all are defensible and documented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate, human_condition_name correctly set), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.
